goals.title: Manding for Preferred Items
goals.description: Child will independently request preferred items or activities using words, signs, or picture exchange
goals.targetBehavior: Child will say or sign 'I want [item]' or exchange a picture to request

teachingProcedures.instruction: Present a highly preferred item within view but out of reach
teachingProcedures.note: Wait 3-5 seconds for a spontaneous request

teachingProcedures.instruction: If no response, provide a verbal prompt: 'What do you want?'
teachingProcedures.note: Pause 2-3 seconds after prompting

teachingProcedures.instruction: If still no response, model the correct request: 'Say I want [item]'
teachingProcedures.note: Use an enthusiastic, encouraging tone

teachingProcedures.instruction: Immediately provide the item when child makes any attempt to request
teachingProcedures.note: Reinforce all attempts, even approximations

teachingProcedures.instruction: Gradually fade prompts over time as child becomes more independent
teachingProcedures.note: Goal is spontaneous requesting without prompts

promptLevels: Physical guidance (if using signs or pictures)
dataTips: Track number of independent requests per day
Record what level of prompting was needed
Note which items motivate the most requests
Track progress toward spontaneous requesting
parentTips: Use only highly preferred items your child really wants
Be consistent - always wait for a request before giving the item
Celebrate all attempts, even if not perfect
Practice throughout the day during natural routines
Keep sessions short and fun (2-5 minutes)
materials: Data sheet or notes app on phone

goals.title: Expanding Vocabulary
goals.description: Child will learn and use new functional words in daily routines
goals.targetBehavior: Child will use 10-20 new functional words consistently

teachingProcedures.instruction: Choose 3-5 highly functional words to start (e.g., more, help, open)
teachingProcedures.note: Pick words the child needs throughout the day

teachingProcedures.instruction: Create natural opportunities to practice each word multiple times daily
teachingProcedures.note: Practice during meals, play, and routines

teachingProcedures.instruction: Model the target word clearly before the child needs it
teachingProcedures.note: Say the word slowly and clearly

teachingProcedures.instruction: Prompt child to repeat the word before providing what they need
teachingProcedures.note: Accept approximations initially

teachingProcedures.instruction: Reinforce by immediately providing the requested item or action
teachingProcedures.note: Pair with praise: 'Great asking for help!'

promptLevels: Expectant pause (wait with item in hand)
dataTips: List target words and track mastery
Note if word was used spontaneously or prompted
Record contexts where word is used
Track generalization across settings
parentTips: Start with words child is motivated to use
Practice the same words consistently for 1-2 weeks
Use words in many different contexts
Exaggerate your pronunciation to make it clear
Be patient - learning new words takes time

goals.title: Answering Questions
goals.description: Child will answer simple 'wh' questions about themselves and their environment
goals.targetBehavior: Child will answer 'what', 'who', and 'where' questions with 80% accuracy

teachingProcedures.instruction: Start with 'what' questions about visible items
teachingProcedures.note: Example: 'What is this?' while holding a ball

teachingProcedures.instruction: Ask the question and immediately provide the answer as a model
teachingProcedures.note: 'What is this? Ball!' - pause for child to repeat

teachingProcedures.instruction: Gradually increase the pause before providing the answer
teachingProcedures.note: Give child more time to respond independently

teachingProcedures.instruction: Reinforce correct responses with praise and access to the item
teachingProcedures.note: 'Yes! It's a ball! Here you go!'

teachingProcedures.instruction: Introduce new question types (who, where) once 'what' is mastered
teachingProcedures.note: Build complexity gradually

dataTips: Track percentage correct by question type
Note what prompts were needed
Record if child generalizes to new examples
Track response time (independence increases as time decreases)
parentTips: Practice during natural activities (e.g., snack time, play)
Start with items child knows well
Use a playful, conversational tone
Mix in mastered and new questions
Keep practice brief and positive

goals.title: Cooperative Play
goals.description: Child will engage in cooperative play activities with peers or siblings
goals.targetBehavior: Child will take turns and share materials during play for at least 5 minutes

teachingProcedures.instruction: Choose a highly motivating activity that requires turn-taking
teachingProcedures.note: Examples: simple board games, building blocks, ball play

teachingProcedures.instruction: Model turn-taking: 'My turn... Your turn!'
teachingProcedures.note: Use clear verbal and visual cues

teachingProcedures.instruction: Provide immediate praise when child waits for their turn
teachingProcedures.note: 'Great waiting! Now it's your turn!'

teachingProcedures.instruction: Keep initial turns very short to maintain engagement
teachingProcedures.note: 5-10 seconds per turn at first

teachingProcedures.instruction: Gradually increase turn length as child becomes more comfortable
teachingProcedures.note: Goal is natural back-and-forth interaction

dataTips: Time the duration of cooperative play
Count number of successful turn exchanges
Note any challenging behaviors
Track improvement over sessions
parentTips: Start with 1-on-1 play before adding peers
Use a timer for clear turn boundaries
Choose activities your child loves
Praise specific behaviors: 'I love how you shared!'
End on a positive note before frustration builds

goals.title: Eye Contact During Interaction
goals.description: Child will make eye contact when greeting others or during conversation
goals.targetBehavior: Child will look at speaker's face for 1-2 seconds when spoken to

teachingProcedures.instruction: Position yourself at child's eye level
teachingProcedures.note: Sit or kneel to be face-to-face

teachingProcedures.instruction: Say child's name and wait for eye contact before continuing
teachingProcedures.note: Only proceed when you have their attention

teachingProcedures.instruction: Immediately reinforce eye contact with praise or preferred item
teachingProcedures.note: 'I love how you looked at me!'

teachingProcedures.instruction: If needed, hold a preferred item near your eyes to encourage looking
teachingProcedures.note: Fade this prompt over time

teachingProcedures.instruction: Practice in natural contexts: greetings, conversations, during play
teachingProcedures.note: Build it into daily routines

dataTips: Track percentage of times child makes eye contact when name is called
Measure duration of eye contact (goal: 1-2 seconds)
Note contexts where eye contact is easiest/hardest
Track reduction in prompts needed
parentTips: Don't force eye contact - make it rewarding
Keep demands brief at first
Use a gentle, friendly tone
Celebrate small wins
Respect if child needs breaks

goals.title: Sharing and Turn-Taking
goals.description: Child will share toys and materials with others during play
goals.targetBehavior: Child will give toys to others when asked and accept toys when offered

teachingProcedures.instruction: Start with less preferred items to reduce difficulty
teachingProcedures.note: Build success before using favorite toys

teachingProcedures.instruction: Model sharing: 'I'm sharing the blocks with you!'
teachingProcedures.note: Narrate your actions

teachingProcedures.instruction: Prompt child to share with you or a sibling
teachingProcedures.note: 'Can you share the car with me?'

teachingProcedures.instruction: Immediately return the item and provide praise
teachingProcedures.note: 'Thank you for sharing! Here it is back!'

teachingProcedures.instruction: Gradually increase to more preferred items as skill develops
teachingProcedures.note: Build tolerance for sharing favorite things

dataTips: Count successful sharing instances per session
Note if sharing was prompted or spontaneous
Track emotional response (calm vs. upset)
Record which items are easier to share
parentTips: Always return items quickly at first
Use a timer so child knows they'll get item back
Praise generously for sharing
Practice with you before expecting peer sharing
Model sharing in your own interactions
materials: Variety of toys (less preferred to start)

goals.title: Toilet Training
goals.description: Child will use the toilet independently for urination and bowel movements
goals.targetBehavior: Child will recognize need to use toilet, go independently, and complete routine with minimal prompting

teachingProcedures.instruction: Establish a regular schedule (every 30-60 minutes initially)
teachingProcedures.note: Use timers as reminders

teachingProcedures.instruction: Prompt child to sit on toilet for 2-3 minutes
teachingProcedures.note: Make it comfortable with books or songs

teachingProcedures.instruction: Provide immediate, enthusiastic reinforcement for any success
teachingProcedures.note: Use special rewards for toilet successes

teachingProcedures.instruction: Teach the full routine: enter bathroom, pull down pants, sit, wipe, flush, wash hands
teachingProcedures.note: Use visual schedule for steps

teachingProcedures.instruction: Gradually increase time between prompted bathroom visits
teachingProcedures.note: Goal is child-initiated toileting

dataTips: Track successful vs. unsuccessful attempts
Note times of day for patterns
Record if child initiated or was prompted
Track accidents
Celebrate progress weekly
parentTips: Stay positive and patient - this takes time
Never punish accidents
Use highly motivating rewards
Dress child in easy-to-remove clothing
Maintain consistency across caregivers
Consult pediatrician if concerns arise
materials: Child-size potty or toilet seat insert

goals.title: Self-Feeding with Utensils
goals.description: Child will feed themselves using a spoon and fork during meals
goals.targetBehavior: Child will independently use utensils to eat most of their meal

teachingProcedures.instruction: Start with easy-to-scoop foods (yogurt, mashed potatoes)
teachingProcedures.note: Success builds confidence

teachingProcedures.instruction: Use hand-over-hand guidance to demonstrate scooping and bringing to mouth
teachingProcedures.note: Practice the full motion

teachingProcedures.instruction: Gradually reduce physical support as child gains motor control
teachingProcedures.note: Move from full hand-over-hand to touching elbow to no touch

teachingProcedures.instruction: Praise each successful bite taken with utensil
teachingProcedures.note: 'Great job using your spoon!'

teachingProcedures.instruction: Introduce fork once spoon is mastered, starting with soft foods
teachingProcedures.note: Progress gradually

dataTips: Count number of bites taken independently
Track percentage of meal eaten with utensils
Note which foods are easier/harder
Record prompting level needed
parentTips: Use toddler-friendly utensils with easy grips
Expect mess - it's part of learning!
Practice when child is hungry but not overly so
Allow extra time for meals
Let child see family members using utensils
Celebrate effort, not just success

goals.title: Independent Dressing
goals.description: Child will put on and remove simple clothing items independently
goals.targetBehavior: Child will complete dressing routine with minimal assistance

teachingProcedures.instruction: Start with easiest items: pull-on pants, slip-on shoes
teachingProcedures.note: Avoid buttons, zippers, and laces initially

teachingProcedures.instruction: Use backward chaining: you do most steps, child completes last step
teachingProcedures.note: Example: you put pants to knees, child pulls up

teachingProcedures.instruction: Provide immediate praise for completing the final step
teachingProcedures.note: 'You did it! You pulled up your pants!'

teachingProcedures.instruction: Gradually add more steps for child to complete
teachingProcedures.note: Work backward through the sequence

teachingProcedures.instruction: Eventually child completes entire sequence independently
teachingProcedures.note: Provide support only as needed

dataTips: Track which steps child can do independently
Note prompting level for each step
Record time to complete (decreases with mastery)
Celebrate when new steps are mastered
parentTips: Choose loose, simple clothing
Practice when not rushed
Use backward chaining for success
Mark clothing to show front/back
Make it fun with songs or games
Allow child to choose clothes when possible

goals.title: Following One-Step Directions
goals.description: Child will follow simple one-step directions consistently
goals.targetBehavior: Child will comply with simple directions within 5 seconds, 80% of the time

teachingProcedures.instruction: Get child's attention first (say name, make eye contact)
teachingProcedures.note: Don't give direction until you have attention

teachingProcedures.instruction: Give clear, simple direction: 'Sit down' or 'Give me the ball'
teachingProcedures.note: One direction at a time, avoid chains

teachingProcedures.instruction: Wait 3-5 seconds for compliance
teachingProcedures.note: Count silently to avoid repeating too soon

teachingProcedures.instruction: If no response, use a prompt (model or physical guidance)
teachingProcedures.note: Help child be successful

teachingProcedures.instruction: Immediately reinforce compliance with praise or preferred activity
teachingProcedures.note: 'Great listening! Let's play!'

dataTips: Track percentage of directions followed independently
Note which types of directions are easiest
Record time to compliance
Track reduction in prompts needed
parentTips: Be clear and consistent with your words
Don't give directions you won't follow through on
Start with easy, preferred directions
Avoid repeating directions multiple times
Make compliance rewarding
Practice throughout the day

goals.title: Smooth Transitions
goals.description: Child will transition between activities without resistance or challenging behavior
goals.targetBehavior: Child will stop current activity and begin new activity within 1 minute when cued

teachingProcedures.instruction: Give a 5-minute warning before transition
teachingProcedures.note: 'In 5 minutes, it's time for bath'

teachingProcedures.instruction: Provide a 1-minute warning
teachingProcedures.note: Use timer so child can see time passing

teachingProcedures.instruction: Clearly state the transition: 'Time to stop playing. Time for bath.'
teachingProcedures.note: Keep language simple and positive

teachingProcedures.instruction: Offer a choice or preview something positive about next activity
teachingProcedures.note: 'You can bring your toy car to bath!'

teachingProcedures.instruction: Immediately reinforce smooth transitions with praise
teachingProcedures.note: 'Wow! Great job stopping and coming to the bath!'

dataTips: Track time from cue to transition completion
Note any challenging behaviors during transitions
Record which transitions are most difficult
Track improvement over time
parentTips: Use visual timers child can see
Give warnings before ending preferred activities
Stay calm and consistent
Build in transition time - don't rush
Offer choices when possible
Praise smooth transitions enthusiastically
materials: Transition objects (item child can carry between activities)

goals.title: Waiting Appropriately
goals.description: Child will wait for desired items or activities without engaging in challenging behavior
goals.targetBehavior: Child will wait calmly for up to 30 seconds when asked

teachingProcedures.instruction: Start with very brief waits (3-5 seconds)
teachingProcedures.note: Build tolerance gradually

teachingProcedures.instruction: Say 'Wait' and show visual cue (hand up or wait card)
teachingProcedures.note: Clear, consistent cue

teachingProcedures.instruction: Immediately provide item/activity after short wait
teachingProcedures.note: Reinforce the waiting behavior

teachingProcedures.instruction: Gradually increase wait time: 5s, 10s, 15s, 20s, 30s
teachingProcedures.note: Progress based on child's success

teachingProcedures.instruction: Praise waiting behavior: 'Great waiting! Here you go!'
teachingProcedures.note: Make waiting worth it

dataTips: Track maximum wait time achieved
Note any challenging behaviors during wait
Record percentage of successful waits
Track increase in tolerance over time
parentTips: Start with very short waits
Use a visual timer child can see
Practice during low-stakes moments first
Always follow through - provide item after wait
Gradually increase difficulty
Celebrate patience!
